sentences.text: All right.
I actually shot pictures inside the courtroom for my own [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] enjoyment and fun or recollection, memory, memorabilia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But oftentimes I had to take the pictures, or most of the time, from the projection booth, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from where I projected the films or the stills [INAUDIBLE].
And one of those pictures I took was of the defendants.
And it so happened that you only can go to-- when you are in a closed room, you cannot get the angle you always want.
So I took this particular picture of the defendants.
And Goering is covered up, except for his belly sticking out, by one of the guards.
And it was a pretty good picture otherwise.
So through connections, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I mentioned before, I never went in there, had anything to do with the defendants.
But the guard that guarded Hoffmann had the connection with the other guards, and he took this picture.
I had it blown up.
I blew it up.
And they're all signed except-- autographed it, except Hess.
But Goering, Schacht, and Franz Papen said to this guard that they didn't want to sign with the rest of the hoodlums on the same paper.
They took out a little three by five card and wrote their names on it.
And I pasted them to the back of the picture, so that in such a way that can be flipped up, and the names underneath are fully visible.
It was my camera, and I took the picture.
From the projection booth.
I don't know.
I didn't agree with the judges to let Franz Papen go free.
He was the, in my opinion, the person who engineered Hitler's foreign policy in there, by the taking over of the countries around Germany, of occupied Europe.
Granted, he may not have had anything to do with the atrocities as such, but he was the direct cause that they were committed, by subjugating those.
And he was just about the main diplomat who really engineered all these.
Well, he was about my size, a little older, of course, than I am.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He looked like a man on the street.
I mean, his clothes were not the best, because he had to, as I mentioned before, he had the GI clothing.
What would he look like?
He was smart as a whip, intelligent.
He did not talk much about his association with Hitler, which I learned much later, in a book that I read just recently, was much closer than I had anticipated at that time.
I had no way of knowing.
Otherwise, I could have asked him, but I didn't know.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was smooth, real smooth.
And we talked about this and that, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nothing really, you know, world-shaking.
No, we never discussed it.
But he was one of the nine original party members.
So that when the party was formed, way back in 1919, he was one of the original nine.
And so he must have been a pretty staunch supporter.
I never touched that field with him.
And maybe I was too timid to ask.
I don't know.
Yeah, he had.
In fact, one of the accused, Baldur von Schirach was his son-in-law.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was very tied up about his fate when his particular case came up.
That much, I remember, yes.
No, that's the only time I asked for her-- I asked him about her.
Yeah, she was his employee.
How well he knew her personally, I have no idea.
But she was a pretty lady.
I have a picture of hers with Hitler.
I think that it's in on my table, the 50th birthday of Hitler.
She's quite a pretty lady.
He was under arrest by the Allied forces.
And the Germans held a trial for him.
It was a German trial.
And they convicted him of war profiteering.
He was tremendously rich.
And what of his personal belongings he could keep later on, I don't know.
But he was sentenced to 10 years of prison, of which he actually served five.
And then he was released.
I guess he was getting too old and feeble, that they couldn't-- but he has two sons, who were still alive in Germany and took over his business.
And then just recently, in one of the magazines or papers, I read where they had a tremendous exhibition of Heinrich Hoffmann's pictures.
Not really.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He knew that I had been born in Germany, of course.
My German gives that away.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But not-- we didn't go too personal either at any time, as far as I can remember.
They were nearing the end, yeah.
Oh, now I remember the name.
Fritzsche was the other guy, the German propaganda guy, that took Goebel's place.
Fritzsche.
It just came back to me.
Yeah, several weeks were left yet, but--

Well, as I mentioned before, when I was seated near this witness Ohlendorf, an SS General Ohlendorf, when he mentioned in the trials, as a witness, that more than five million Jews met the final solution, or the final solution was applied to more than five million Jews.
That was a pretty-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] pretty hard-hitting moment.
When did I what?
I don't remember.
I have to look it up.
I really don't remember.
I went by train to Bremerhaven, and I took a boat there, and back to the United States.
Well, I was already discharged.
Yeah, I took my discharge.
As a civilian.
So you were paid as a civilian.
As a civilian.
Yes.
Oh, they all want-- everybody wanted to know, of course.
And I showed my pictures to everybody who wanted to see them.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I now take great delight, actual delight, in going to the schools, showing my documentary, which, for the schools, has been edited a little bit, to make it shorter, to make it fit into the time frame, and also to leave out some of the irrelevant talks that take place in there.
But it gives me a great satisfaction to teach today's generation that these things are not inventions, that they actually happened.
And when you show this picture, especially when the crowd is pretty harsh and awful, sudden this picture appears with a woman with her breast cut off, oh!
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The whole crowd.
Yes, as long as their time permits.
I have no time limit.
Some are pretty relevant.
Other ones are childish.
But they ask me much how did you get these pictures?
What are your personal feelings about it?
Other ones are a little bit more philosophical.
And what do I think should be done, could be done?
And I say, what could be done is what I'm doing now, or should be done.
Show you this.
But I had an incident last year.
It was really a highlight.
I was showing this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I not only show, but I also give a lecture.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after it was all over, I was told by the teacher in charge that there were two German exchange students.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me.
And I talked with them briefly after the lecture.
And one of them said, you know, mister-- we were talking German.
You are the first Jew I ever talked to.
And I talked to the teacher, and I suggested to have these two and the teacher and her husband, who's also a teacher, come over for a cookout here.
And we did.
And in the meantime, I had copies made of the these, of my tape.
And I gave the students the copy.
That's the one you saw.
KNME-TV, Channel 5, here in Albuquerque.
I think they're the most unreasonable and far out persons that could ever live.
I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] think-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they leave me speechless.
How can they deny something like that, that so many of us have experienced?
Yeah, about 13 years ago, my son was stationed in Germany in the Air Force.
And he took us, Alicia and me, on a trip through Germany.
And we spent just a couple of hours in Nuremberg.
It was so rebuilt, I couldn't recognize it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was so-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we also went, on that same trip, to a little place called Mittenwald, which is famous for the violins that are being built there.
And it so happened that, during the war, we took that place, our regiment.
And I was the one who interrupted the negotiation, the surrender negotiations, for that little place.
With the exception of Franz Papen I think I was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] rest of them-- Fritzsche I'm sure should not have been blamed.
He shouldn't even have been in the trial, because he was a newcomer.
He had only been in the job six weeks.
You know, it's well-known that Goebbels was the former minister of propaganda.
When he realized that the situation became untenable, he shot his wife and seven children and then shot himself.
And Fritzsche was the one who took Goebbels' place.
But I don't think he had anything to do with the-- and he was acquitted, and I felt that was correct.
Right.
I figured they were getting old enough, where they were harmless, really harmless, at the time.
I mean, what.
And just, for instance, I was against Hess being held in Spandau, where we had to have the pretty near military establishment just for him.
I thought it useless.
Not that his sentence wasn't appropriate, but I felt, at that time, it was a useless undertaking.
Lumber.
I went right back into the lumber business.
Yes.
In 1949, I had a chance to go to Mexico.
I had an offer down there that paid me twice as much as I was actually receiving here.
And I took it.
And while in Mexico, I met my now wife, Alicia.
Moreno.
And in 1951, we got married, on the 4th of May.
And it was quite a courtship.
And we have four wonderful children, two boys and two girls.
Norman Randolph, Robert Edward, Lillian, and Else Arlene.
And they're all professionals and married to professionals.
We're very lucky with-- lucky or mother's wisdom in bringing them up.
10 Years.
We came back to the States, because the last job I held, it was the best job I ever held in my life.
But the climate was of such a nature, that we feared for the health of the children.
And this was a lumber camp that was way out in the sticks.
And there was no doctor there, no school teacher.

And my wife, even though she was a nurse anesthetist in her profession, in her profession-- but we did not particularly care, especially she, with the children, didn't particularly care to live in this place.
We went back to the company where I left from.
Came right back.
The legacy-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I personally hope that nothing like that or even remotely approaching it should ever happen again.
And the [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] lesson that I feel should be taken is that we should respect each other as human beings and not divide each other by racial or religious groups.
And I think my wife, who is a staunch Catholic, and I am a good Jew, we will be married in June for 45 years.
And I think [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that proves my point.
The only one here in town is Marvin Atlas, who was on the prosecution staff, who was a lawyer.
But I did not know him during the trial days.
I never had any contact with him.
And he's about the only one.
There is a lady, I told you before.
Lizzie [INAUDIBLE] was a secretary, but whom I did not know.
In Washington.
It was quite a reunion of some of the details that came out, for instance, about Hitler's death, that I didn't know about; Goering's death, how did he get the capsule.
And the doctor, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think Dr.
Lattimer, ?] told me-- or told us as a group, that Goering, while he was prisoner, always complained about chapped lips.
And his skin was all red.
He had a rash.

And the guards became so used to his going to the other room, wherever it was, to get his [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] skin ointments, or whatever it was, that they didn't realize that that was the place where he kept his cyanide capsule.
See, they had been bodily searched everywhere in the room, searched daily for cyanide, for the capsules.
And that's where he kept it, and he dug it out of this jar.
And that's how he, a few minutes before being, supposed to have been, hanged, got his jar out of there, bit it, and of course, died.
Just about two weeks ago.
Yeah.

Another thing I learned that I hadn't known is that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I always had thought that after Hitler married Eva Braun, that he gave orders to his troops that when he and Eva were to leave the bunker where there were, to shoot them and then douse their bodies with gasoline and burn them.
But this same doctor told me something completely different.
Hitler actually used a cyanide capsule, bit it, and the second he bit it, also fired a pistol into-- a shot into his head.
So he had-- he wanted to make sure he'd die.
And then I didn't ask how Eva Braun died, but the troops actually killed her or she committed suicide.
But then the troops doused their bodies with gasoline.
And the Russians were supposed to have picked up-- I mean, denied it for a long time.
But they had picked up the bodies.
And the reason they could identify the burned bodies, the Americans, later on, because they had dental x-rays of Hitler's.
And they matched the skull x-rays with the x-rays they had.
I wouldn't know what to change.
I lived a happy and full and a very complete life, a wonderful family.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was lucky.
Maybe one thing I could-- would try to do-- I didn't have a chance while I was younger-- to go to college.
But I barely finished high school in 1964, at night classes, when I had already my family.
But that may be the only thing.
But I am happy.
I would like to tell those people who look in or see this or see the evidence to be aware that racial and religious strife is one of the worst things that can happen to humanity.
And we see it right now in Yugoslavia and have seen it in many other parts of the world since.
And that's it.
You're welcome.
